title: Custom Visualization with ggplot2 and plotly
text: ’s simulation tibble looks like: row one body one point time. Every column available plotting, filtering, analysis. Since just tibble, full power dplyr ggplot2 disposal.

title: Custom Visualization with ggplot2 and plotly
text: example, Earth-Moon system, plot_orbits() shows overlapping circles bodies orbit shared barycenter roughly scale. useful visualization might plot body’s distance barycenter time:  plot Moon’s path relative Earth color gradient showing passage time:
code: library(ggplot2)  sim |>   dplyr::mutate(r = sqrt(x^2 + y^2)) |>   ggplot(aes(x = time / 86400, y = r, color = id)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 1) +   labs(     title = "Distance from Barycenter Over Time",     x = "Time (days)",     y = "Distance (m)",     color = "Body"   ) +   theme_minimal() sim |>   shift_reference_frame("Earth", keep_center = FALSE) |>   ggplot(aes(x = x, y = y, color = time / 86400)) +   geom_path(linewidth = 1.2) +   scale_color_viridis_c(name = "Day") +   coord_equal() +   labs(title = "Lunar Orbit (Earth-Centered)", x = "X (m)", y = "Y (m)") +   theme_minimal()

title: Custom Visualization with ggplot2 and plotly
text: Just plot_orbits() quick convenience 2D work, plot_orbits_3d() quick convenience 3D. intentionally simple — get useful plot one line can focus physics, formatting. need control, simulation tibble works just well plotly ggplot2. example, color trajectories speed rather body: point ggplot2: simulate_system() returns standard tibble, full access plotly’s API anything built-plotting functions don’t cover.
code: library(plotly) #>  #> Attaching package: 'plotly' #> The following object is masked from 'package:ggplot2': #>  #>     last_plot #> The following object is masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     filter #> The following object is masked from 'package:graphics': #>  #>     layout  sim <- create_system() |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon,            x = distance_earth_moon,            vy = speed_moon * cos(5 * pi / 180),            vz = speed_moon * sin(5 * pi / 180)) |>   simulate_system(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 28)  sim <- sim |>   dplyr::mutate(speed = sqrt(vx^2 + vy^2 + vz^2))  plot_ly() |>   add_trace(     data = dplyr::filter(sim, id == "Moon"),     x = ~x, y = ~y, z = ~z,     type = 'scatter3d', mode = 'lines',     line = list(       width = 5,       color = ~speed,       colorscale = 'Viridis',       showscale = TRUE,       colorbar = list(title = "Speed (m/s)")     ),     name = "Moon"   ) |>   add_trace(     data = dplyr::filter(sim, id == "Earth"),     x = ~x, y = ~y, z = ~z,     type = 'scatter3d', mode = 'lines',     line = list(width = 3, color = 'gray'),     name = "Earth"   ) |>   layout(     title = "Lunar Orbit Around Earth",     showlegend = FALSE,     scene = list(       xaxis = list(title = 'X (m)'),       yaxis = list(title = 'Y (m)'),       zaxis = list(title = 'Z (m)'),       aspectmode = "data"     )   )

title: Examples
text: standard 28-day lunar orbit. One-hour time steps.
code: create_system() |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon, x = distance_earth_moon, vy = speed_moon) |>   simulate_system(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 28) |>   plot_orbits()

title: Examples
text: full year daily time steps.
code: create_system() |>   add_body("Sun",   mass = mass_sun) |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth, x = distance_earth_sun, vy = speed_earth) |>   simulate_system(time_step = 86400, duration = 86400 * 365) |>   plot_orbits()

title: Examples
text: orbitr uses N-body gravity, nested hierarchies require special setup. Piggyback Moon’s initial conditions onto Earth’s using simple vector addition. Note scale, Earth Moon orbits overlap — Earth-Moon distance (~384,000 km) tiny compared Earth-Sun distance (~150 million km). Use shift_reference_frame("Earth") (shown next example) zoom Earth-Moon subsystem:
code: create_system() |>   add_body("Sun",   mass = mass_sun) |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth, x = distance_earth_sun, vy = speed_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon,            x = distance_earth_sun + distance_earth_moon,            vy = speed_earth + speed_moon) |>   simulate_system(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 365) |>   plot_orbits()

title: Examples
text: three-body plot heliocentric (Sun center). see Moon’s path Earth’s perspective, pipe results shift_reference_frame():
code: create_system() |>   add_body("Sun",   mass = mass_sun) |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth, x = distance_earth_sun, vy = speed_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon,            x = distance_earth_sun + distance_earth_moon,            vy = speed_earth + speed_moon) |>   simulate_system(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 365) |>   shift_reference_frame("Earth") |>   plot_orbits()

what: The Kepler-16 System: A Real Circumbinary Planet
title: Examples
text: Kepler-16b first confirmed planet orbiting two stars — real-life Tatooine. system K-type star (0.68 solar masses) M-type star (0.20 solar masses) orbiting every ~41 days, Saturn-sized planet orbiting pair 0.7 AU.
code: G <- 6.6743e-11 AU <- distance_earth_sun  # Star masses m_A <- 0.68 * mass_sun m_B <- 0.20 * mass_sun m_planet <- 0.333 * mass_jupiter  # Binary star orbit (~0.22 AU separation) a_bin <- 0.22 * AU r_A <- a_bin * m_B / (m_A + m_B) r_B <- a_bin * m_A / (m_A + m_B) v_A <- sqrt(G * m_B^2 / ((m_A + m_B) * a_bin)) v_B <- sqrt(G * m_A^2 / ((m_A + m_B) * a_bin))  # Planet orbit (0.7048 AU from barycenter) r_planet <- 0.7048 * AU v_planet <- sqrt(G * (m_A + m_B) / r_planet)  create_system() |>   add_body("Star A", mass = m_A, x = r_A, vy = v_A) |>   add_body("Star B", mass = m_B, x = -r_B, vy = -v_B) |>   add_body("Kepler-16b", mass = m_planet, x = r_planet, vy = v_planet) |>   simulate_system(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 228.8 * 3) |>   plot_orbits()

title: Built-In Physical Constants
code: mass_sun          # 1.989e30 kg mass_earth        # 5.972e24 kg mass_moon         # 7.342e22 kg mass_mars         # 6.417e23 kg mass_jupiter      # 1.898e27 kg mass_saturn       # 5.683e26 kg mass_venus        # 4.867e24 kg mass_mercury      # 3.301e23 kg

title: Built-In Physical Constants
code: distance_earth_sun    # 1.496e11 m  (~149.6 million km) distance_earth_moon   # 3.844e8  m  (~384,400 km) distance_mars_sun     # 2.279e11 m distance_jupiter_sun  # 7.785e11 m distance_venus_sun    # 1.082e11 m distance_mercury_sun  # 5.791e10 m

title: Built-In Physical Constants
code: speed_earth       # 29,780 m/s speed_moon        #  1,022 m/s speed_mars        # 24,070 m/s speed_jupiter     # 13,060 m/s speed_venus       # 35,020 m/s speed_mercury     # 47,360 m/s

title: Built-In Physical Constants
text: means Earth-Moon example can written :
code: create_system() |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon, x = distance_earth_moon, vy = speed_moon) |>   simulate_system(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 28) |>   plot_orbits()

title: Built-In Physical Constants
text: Orbital distances truly constant — every orbit ellipse, separation two bodies changes continuously throughout revolution. values provided semi-major axes: average closest approach (periapsis) farthest point (apoapsis). semi-major axis single characteristic length scale elliptical orbit. determines orbital period via Kepler’s Third Law, paired mean orbital speed, produces near-circular trajectory closely approximates real orbit. example, Earth-Sun distance varies 147.1 million km January (perihelion) 152.1 million km July (aphelion). semi-major axis 149.6 million km sits right middle gives correct one-year orbital period. want elliptical orbit instead, start body periapsis faster--mean velocity, apoapsis slower one.

title: 3D Plotting
text: plot_orbits() checks whether (sim_data$z != 0). ’s Z-axis movement — pass three_d = TRUE — hands plot_orbits_3d(), uses plotly interactive 3D rendering. Otherwise falls back static ggplot2 chart. plotly isn’t installed, function warns falls back 2D plot even 3D data.

title: 3D Plotting
text: Moon’s real orbit inclined 5° ecliptic. can approximate giving Moon small vz component: vz non-zero, plot_orbits() detects 3D motion returns interactive plotly widget. can drag rotate, scroll zoom, hover see timestamps.
code: create_system() |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon,            x = distance_earth_moon,            vy = speed_moon * cos(5 * pi / 180),            vz = speed_moon * sin(5 * pi / 180)) |>   simulate_system(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 28) |>   plot_orbits()

title: 3D Plotting
text: Even system entirely planar, can opt interactive 3D viewer: gives rotation zoom controls, even though Z values zero.
code: create_system() |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon, x = distance_earth_moon, vy = speed_moon) |>   simulate_system(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 28) |>   plot_orbits(three_d = TRUE)

title: Quick Start Guide
text: 3D interactive plotting, ’ll also want:
code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("daverosenman/orbitr") install.packages("plotly")

title: Quick Start Guide
text: orbitr designed around simple pipe-friendly workflow: create system, add bodies, simulate, plot.  ’s — 28-day lunar orbit four lines. ’s step : create_system() initializes empty simulation standard gravitational constant G. add_body() places body given mass, position, velocity. positions meters, velocities m/s. built-constants (mass_earth, distance_earth_moon, etc.) save looking anything . simulate_system() runs N-body integration forward time. time_step many seconds per integration step, duration total time simulate. plot_orbits() produces quick 2D trajectory plot using ggplot2.
code: library(orbitr)  create_system() |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon, x = distance_earth_moon, vy = speed_moon) |>   simulate_system(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 28) |>   plot_orbits()

title: Quick Start Guide
text: default, plot_orbits() returns standard ggplot object planar (2D) simulations plotly HTML widget simulations 3D motion. (can also force 3D rendering planar data three_d = TRUE.) 2D case returns regular ggplot, can layer additional geoms, scales, themes, labels onto + like ggplot. common annoyance central body two-body system can invisible: plot_orbits() draws body geom_path() trajectory, much massive body barely moves path small see. Sun Sun-Earth simulation classic example — ’s , loop around barycenter well inside Sun . simplest fix drop marker origin:  works Sun sits essentially origin throughout simulation. systems central body actually moves noticeable amount, ’d want pull position simulation tibble instead hardcoding (0, 0).
code: sim <- create_system() |>   add_body("Sun",   mass = mass_sun) |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth, x = distance_earth_sun, vy = speed_earth) |>   simulate_system(time_step = 86400, duration = 86400 * 365)  sim |>   plot_orbits() +   ggplot2::geom_point(     data = data.frame(x = 0, y = 0),     ggplot2::aes(x = x, y = y),     color = "gold",     size = 6   ) +   ggplot2::labs(title = "Earth-Sun Orbit")

title: Quick Start Guide
text: Since orbitr full N-body engine, can add many bodies want. one gravitationally interacts every . ’s Sun-Earth-Moon system full year:  Notice shift_reference_frame("Earth") — re-centers everything Earth can see Moon’s orbit instead everything overlap Sun’s scale.
code: create_system() |>   add_body("Sun",   mass = mass_sun) |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth, x = distance_earth_sun, vy = speed_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon,            x = distance_earth_sun + distance_earth_moon,            vy = speed_earth + speed_moon) |>   simulate_system(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 365) |>   shift_reference_frame("Earth") |>   plot_orbits()

title: Quick Start Guide
text: default integrator Velocity Verlet, conserves energy keeps orbits stable. can switch "euler_cromer" faster (less accurate) runs, "euler" see happens energy isn’t conserved:  ’ll see orbit spiral outward — ’s Euler method artificially pumping energy system. Switch back method = "verlet" clean closed ellipse.
code: create_system() |>   add_body("Star", mass = 1e30) |>   add_body("Planet", mass = 1e24, x = 1e11, vy = 30000) |>   simulate_system(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 365, method = "euler") |>   plot_orbits()

what: The Output is Just a Tibble
title: Quick Start Guide
text: simulate_system() returns standard tidy tibble. can use dplyr, ggplot2, plotly, tool : row one body one point time, columns position (x, y, z), velocity (vx, vy, vz), mass, body ID, time.

title: Quick Start Guide
text: Physics — Understand math behind simulation Examples — complex systems including binary stars Kepler-16 system Unstable Orbits — random configurations chaotic 3D Plotting — Interactive 3D visualization plotly Custom Visualization — Build plots ggplot2 plotly Physical Constants — built-masses, distances, speeds Roadmap — Features considered future versions, plus place suggest

title: Reference Frames
text: Every N-body simulation pick origin — point sits (0, 0, 0). default, orbitr uses coordinate system set : whatever body placed origin stays (least initially), everything else measured relative point. reference frame. trouble “natural” reference frame building simulation isn’t always best one understanding results. Consider Sun-Earth-Moon system. obvious way set heliocentric (Sun origin):  scale Earth-Moon distance (~384,000 km) rounding error compared Earth-Sun distance (~150 million km). Moon’s trajectory overlaps Earth’s completely, Sun barely moves (tiny wobble around barycenter invisible zoom level), plot_orbits() shows looks like single circular track. physics fine; perspective wrong.
code: sim <- create_system() |>   add_body("Sun",   mass = mass_sun) |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth, x = distance_earth_sun, vy = speed_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon,            x = distance_earth_sun + distance_earth_moon,            vy = speed_earth + speed_moon) |>   simulate_system(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 365)  sim |> plot_orbits()

title: Reference Frames
text: shift_reference_frame() fixes applying Galilean coordinate transformation. every time step, subtracts position velocity chosen body bodies: r→′(t)=r→(t)−r→center(t)v→′(t)=v→(t)−v→center(t) \vec{r}'_i(t) = \vec{r}_i(t) - \vec{r}_{\text{center}}(t) \qquad \vec{v}'_i(t) = \vec{v}_i(t) - \vec{v}_{\text{center}}(t) chosen body ends fixed origin, every body’s trajectory shows motion relative body. physics changes — forces, accelerations — ’re just moving camera.  Now Earth sits center, Moon traces familiar near-circular orbit, Sun sweeps wide arc background. geocentric view — system, seen different place.
code: sim |>   shift_reference_frame("Earth") |>   plot_orbits()

title: Reference Frames
text: function signature : transformation operates six phase-space coordinates (x, y, z, vx, vy, vz) simultaneously. time step, function captures center body’s exact state subtracts every body system. result tibble structure input — slots right back pipe.
code: shift_reference_frame(sim_data, center_id, keep_center = TRUE)

what: Keeping vs. Removing the Center Body
title: Reference Frames
text: default, keep_center = TRUE leaves center body output. coordinates zero every time step, appears stationary dot origin. useful want see “camera” . Set keep_center = FALSE drop center body entirely. better choice ’re feeding shifted data custom visualization analysis pipeline don’t need point stuck zero:
code: library(ggplot2)  sim |>   shift_reference_frame("Earth", keep_center = FALSE) |>   dplyr::filter(id == "Moon") |>   ggplot(aes(x = x, y = y, color = time / 86400)) +   geom_path(linewidth = 1.2) +   scale_color_viridis_c(name = "Day") +   coord_equal() +   labs(title = "Lunar Orbit (Earth-Centered)", x = "X (m)", y = "Y (m)") +   theme_minimal()

what: Multiple Perspectives on the Same Data
title: Reference Frames
text: Since shift_reference_frame() doesn’t modify underlying physics — just translates coordinates — can call multiple times simulation explore different viewpoints. ’s need re-run simulate_system().    Moon-centered (selenocentric) view particularly interesting: Moon’s perspective, Earth appears orbit small circle, Sun sweeps much larger arc. course just matter perspective — physics doesn’t care body call center.
code: # Same simulation, three different perspectives  # 1. From the Sun (original frame, but explicit) sim |>   shift_reference_frame("Sun") |>   plot_orbits() # 2. From the Earth sim |>   shift_reference_frame("Earth") |>   plot_orbits() # 3. From the Moon sim |>   shift_reference_frame("Moon") |>   plot_orbits()

title: Reference Frames
text: velocity transformation just useful position transformation. shifting Earth’s frame, velocity columns (vx, vy, vz) give body’s velocity relative Earth. can use study Moon’s orbital speed varies time:  oscillation reflects Moon’s slightly elliptical orbit — speeds perigee (closest approach) slows apogee (farthest point), exactly Kepler’s second law predicts.
code: library(ggplot2)  sim |>   shift_reference_frame("Earth", keep_center = FALSE) |>   dplyr::filter(id == "Moon") |>   dplyr::mutate(speed = sqrt(vx^2 + vy^2 + vz^2)) |>   ggplot(aes(x = time / 86400, y = speed)) +   geom_line(color = "#2563eb", linewidth = 0.8) +   labs(title = "Moon's Orbital Speed Relative to Earth",        x = "Time (days)", y = "Speed (m/s)") +   theme_minimal()

what: A Binary Star System from the Planet’s View
title: Reference Frames
text: Reference frame shifts aren’t limited massive body. can center body system. ’s Kepler-16b — real circumbinary planet — looking back two parent stars: default (barycentric) frame, see planet’s wide orbit stars’ tight inner dance:  Now shift planet’s perspective:  Kepler-16b, stars trace looping spirograph-like patterns — combination binary’s mutual orbit planet’s revolution around pair. double sunset looks like map time: two stars dance around slowly circling sky.
code: G <- 6.6743e-11 AU <- distance_earth_sun  m_A <- 0.68 * mass_sun m_B <- 0.20 * mass_sun m_planet <- 0.333 * mass_jupiter  a_bin <- 0.22 * AU r_A <- a_bin * m_B / (m_A + m_B) r_B <- a_bin * m_A / (m_A + m_B) v_A <- sqrt(G * m_B^2 / ((m_A + m_B) * a_bin)) v_B <- sqrt(G * m_A^2 / ((m_A + m_B) * a_bin))  r_planet <- 0.7048 * AU v_planet <- sqrt(G * (m_A + m_B) / r_planet)  kepler16 <- create_system() |>   add_body("Star A", mass = m_A, x = r_A, vy = v_A) |>   add_body("Star B", mass = m_B, x = -r_B, vy = -v_B) |>   add_body("Kepler-16b", mass = m_planet, x = r_planet, vy = v_planet) |>   simulate_system(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 228.8 * 3) kepler16 |> plot_orbits() kepler16 |>   shift_reference_frame("Kepler-16b", keep_center = FALSE) |>   plot_orbits()

title: Reference Frames
text: Don’t re-simulate. shift_reference_frame() pure coordinate transformation output tibble. ’s fast doesn’t require re-running physics. Store simulation result shift many times need. Use keep_center = FALSE custom plots. piping ggplot2 plotly, center body sitting (0, 0) zero velocity can clutter visualization skew axis ranges. Dropping keeps plots clean. Chain dplyr. output standard tibble, can filter, mutate, summarize shifting. instance, compute distance two bodies time, track relative velocity evolves:  frame doesn’t affect physics. Shifting reference frame post-processing step. gravitational forces, accelerations, numerical integration happened original coordinate system. ’re just choosing stand look results.
code: # Distance between Earth and Moon over time sim |>   shift_reference_frame("Earth", keep_center = FALSE) |>   dplyr::filter(id == "Moon") |>   dplyr::mutate(distance_km = sqrt(x^2 + y^2 + z^2) / 1000) |>   ggplot(aes(x = time / 86400, y = distance_km)) +   geom_line(color = "#dc2626", linewidth = 0.8) +   labs(title = "Earth-Moon Distance Over One Year",        x = "Time (days)", y = "Distance (km)") +   theme_minimal()

title: Roadmap
text: current plot_orbits() plot_orbits_3d() functions draw trajectories — full path body sweeps simulation. complementary pair functions, plot_system() plot_system_3d(), render snapshot system single time step: body dot (sized mass, radius one supplied), optionally velocity vectors arrows. Useful debugging initial conditions, comparing state different times, building frames animation.

title: Roadmap
text: Something like animate_orbits() wraps gganimate (writes GIF/MP4 directly) play simulation forward time. tibble output simulate_system() already right shape — ’s just matter wiring clean wrapper users don’t write boilerplate .

title: Roadmap
text: Right now bodies treated point masses. fine orbital distances — gravity outside sphere behaves exactly mass concentrated center (shell theorem) — means ’s concept two bodies physically touching. Adding optional radius parameter enable collision detection, merging contact, realistic close-encounter behavior. ’d probably add add_body() optional parameter sensible default, something like: r supplied body system, integrator check overlaps step handle according user-chosen policy (elastic bounce, inelastic merge, simulation halt, etc.). meantime, existing softening parameter simulate_system() partly works around missing-radius problem preventing gravitational force blowing small separations — see Physics details.
code: add_body <- function(system, id, mass, x = 0, y = 0, z = 0,                      vx = 0, vy = 0, vz = 0, r = NULL)

title: Roadmap
text: Optional support forces beyond pure Newtonian gravity: Atmospheric drag low orbits around bodies atmospheres Radiation pressure small bodies near star J2 oblateness corrections orbits around non-spherical bodies (Earth’s equatorial bulge measurably perturbs satellite orbits)

title: Roadmap
text: small post-Newtonian correction term let orbitr reproduce real GR effects like precession Mercury’s perihelion. Probably opt-via argument simulate_system(), since users wouldn’t need .

what: Construct bodies from Keplerian orbital elements
title: Roadmap
text: current add_body() takes raw Cartesian state vectors (x, y, z, vx, vy, vz). users think classical orbital elements — semi-major axis aa, eccentricity ee, inclination ii, longitude ascending node Ω\Omega, argument periapsis ω\omega, true anomaly ν\nu — helper converts right Cartesian inputs quality--life win:
code: add_body_keplerian(system, "Mars", mass = mass_mars,                    a = distance_mars_sun, e = 0.0934,                    i = 1.85, Omega = 49.6, omega = 286.5, nu = 0,                    parent = "Sun")

title: Roadmap
text: one-liner pre-builds Sun eight planets real ephemeris values, can skip straight interesting experiments without typing dozen add_body() calls.

title: Roadmap
text: Functions serialize orbit_system (completed simulation tibble) disk reload later. Useful long-running simulations sharing reproducible setups.

title: Roadmap
text: simple progress indicator simulate_system() runs take seconds, option disable scripted use.

title: Roadmap
text: Helpers compute total energy, total linear momentum, total angular momentum every time step, can sanity-check integrator’s behavior course run. Useful confirming Velocity Verlet job, spotting cases time step large.

title: Roadmap
text: sound useful, ’d like see something list, use case orbitr doesn’t currently handle well, please open issue GitHub. ’d love hear people using package make useful. Pull requests also welcome.

title: The Physics
text: Every body system attracts every body according Newton’s Law Universal Gravitation. body jj, net acceleration due bodies kk : →j=∑k≠jGmkrjk2r̂jk\vec{}_j = \sum_{k \neq j} \frac{G \, m_k}{r_{jk}^2} \, \hat{r}_{jk} rjk=|r→k−r→j|r_{jk} = \lvert\vec{r}_k - \vec{r}_j\rvert distance two bodies r̂jk\hat{r}_{jk} unit vector pointing jj toward kk.

title: The Physics
text: Gravity alone pull every body straight toward every body. prevents colliding initial velocity — sideways motion turns free-fall curved orbit. reason Moon doesn’t crash Earth: ’s falling toward us constantly, ’s also moving sideways fast enough keeps missing. call add_body(), vx, vy, vz parameters set initial velocity. balance speed distance determines shape orbit. given distance rr central mass MM, circular orbit velocity : vcirc=GMrv_{\text{circ}} = \sqrt{\frac{G \, M}{r}} body’s speed exactly matches , traces perfect circle. Faster orbit stretches ellipse (escapes entirely v≥vcirc2v \geq v_{\text{circ}} \sqrt{2}). Slower orbit drops tighter ellipse dips closer central body. zero velocity, body falls straight — orbit .

title: The Physics
text: two bodies pass close, r→0r \0 acceleration diverges toward infinity. well-known numerical problem N-body codes. orbitr offers optional softening length ε\varepsilon regularizes potential: rsoft=r2+ε2r_{\text{soft}} = \sqrt{r^2 + \varepsilon^2} softening enabled, close encounters produce large finite forces instead blowing NaN. Set softening = 0 (default) exact Newtonian gravity, try something like softening = 1e4 (10 km) dense systems.

title: The Physics
text: simulate_system() offers three methods stepping system forward time. operate 3D Cartesian coordinates.

title: The Physics
text: second-order symplectic integrator. conserves energy long timescales, making gold standard orbital mechanics. Orbits stay closed stable indefinitely. x→t+Δt=x→t+v→tΔt+12a→tΔt2\vec{x}_{t+\Delta t} = \vec{x}_t + \vec{v}_t \, \Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2} \vec{}_t \, \Delta t^2 v→t+Δt=v→t+12(→t+→t+Δt)Δt\vec{v}_{t+\Delta t} = \vec{v}_t + \tfrac{1}{2} \left( \vec{}_t + \vec{}_{t+\Delta t} \right) \Delta t position advanced first, acceleration recalculated new position, finally velocity updated using average old new accelerations. requires two acceleration evaluations per step (main cost), payoff stability enormous.

title: The Physics
text: first-order symplectic method. updates velocity first, uses new velocity update position. small reordering prevents systematic energy drift plagues standard Euler: v→t+Δt=v→t+→tΔt\vec{v}_{t+\Delta t} = \vec{v}_t + \vec{}_t \, \Delta t x→t+Δt=x→t+v→t+ΔtΔt\vec{x}_{t+\Delta t} = \vec{x}_t + \vec{v}_{t+\Delta t} \, \Delta t Faster Verlet (one acceleration evaluation per step) less accurate. Good quick previews.

title: The Physics
text: classical textbook method. Position velocity updated using values current time step: x→t+Δt=x→t+v→tΔt\vec{x}_{t+\Delta t} = \vec{x}_t + \vec{v}_t \, \Delta t v→t+Δt=v→t+→tΔt\vec{v}_{t+\Delta t} = \vec{v}_t + \vec{}_t \, \Delta t artificially pumps energy system, causing orbits spiral outward time. Included primarily educational comparison — use Verlet real work.

title: The Physics
text: Verlet traces clean closed ellipse, Euler-Cromer stays close drifts slightly, standard Euler spirals outward pumps energy orbit.
code: library(orbitr) library(dplyr) #>  #> Attaching package: 'dplyr' #> The following objects are masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union  system <- create_system() |>   add_body("Star", mass = 1e30) |>   add_body("Planet", mass = 1e24, x = 1e11, vy = 30000)  verlet <- simulate_system(system, time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 365, method = "verlet") |>   mutate(method = "Velocity Verlet")  euler_cromer <- simulate_system(system, time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 365, method = "euler_cromer") |>   mutate(method = "Euler-Cromer")  euler <- simulate_system(system, time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 365, method = "euler") |>   mutate(method = "Standard Euler")  bind_rows(verlet, euler_cromer, euler) |>   filter(id == "Planet") |>   ggplot2::ggplot(ggplot2::aes(x = x, y = y, color = method)) +   ggplot2::geom_path(alpha = 0.7) +   ggplot2::coord_equal() +   ggplot2::theme_minimal()

title: The Physics
text: inner acceleration loop computational bottleneck N-body simulation. orbitr ships compiled C++ kernel (via Rcpp) computes O(n2)O(n^2) pairwise interactions tight nested loop. package installed source working C++ toolchain, simulate_system() automatically dispatches engine. compiled code isn’t available, falls back vectorized R implementation uses matrix outer products — still fast, C++ path significantly faster systems many bodies. can control use_cpp argument:
code: # Force the pure-R engine (useful for debugging or benchmarking) simulate_system(system, use_cpp = FALSE)

title: Unstable Orbits and the Three-Body Problem
text: ’s example: three equal-mass stars arranged triangle slightly asymmetric velocities. starts looking like interesting dance, asymmetry compounds eventually one stars get ejected:  actually happens real stellar dynamics — close three-body encounters star clusters frequently eject one star high velocity remaining two settle tighter binary. process called gravitational slingshot ejection.
code: create_system() |>   add_body("Star A", mass = 1e30, x = 1e11, y = 0, vx = 0, vy = 15000) |>   add_body("Star B", mass = 1e30, x = -5e10, y = 8.66e10, vx = -12990, vy = -7500) |>   add_body("Star C", mass = 1e30, x = -5e10, y = -8.66e10, vx = 14000, vy = -8000) |>   simulate_system(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 365 * 10) |>   plot_orbits()

title: Unstable Orbits and the Three-Body Problem
text: simulations producing messy, diverging trajectories, things check assuming something wrong: Velocity high low. given distance rr central mass MM, circular orbit speed v=GM/rv = \sqrt{GM/r}. Deviating significantly produces eccentric orbits escape trajectories. Bodies close together. Close encounters produce extreme accelerations can blow numerically. Try increasing softening using smaller time_step. Three bodies. Chaos natural state N-body systems. stable examples documentation carefully tuned — don’t expect random configurations behave. Time step large. bodies move significant fraction orbital radius single step, integrator can’t track orbit accurately. Try halving time_step see result changes. built-constants examples orbitr designed give stable starting points. can tweak parameters watch system responds — ’s real intuition orbital mechanics comes .

code: @Manual{,   title = {orbitr: A Tidy Physics Engine for Building and Visualizing Orbital Simulations},   author = {Dave Rosenman},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.1.0},   url = {https://drosenman.github.io/orbitr/}, }

what: A Tidy Physics Engine for Building and Visualizing Orbital Simulations
title: A Tidy Physics Engine for Building and Visualizing Orbital Simulations
text: tidy physics engine building visualizing orbital simulations. Note: orbitr work progress. physics engine functional, built-plotting functions (plot_orbits() plot_orbits_3d()) intentionally minimal — exist get quick look simulation, produce publication-quality figures. Since simulate_system() returns standard tidy tibble, full power ggplot2, plotly, visualization library disposal. See Custom Visualization examples. orbitr lightweight N-body gravitational physics engine built R ecosystem. Simulate planetary orbits, binary star systems, chaotic three-body problems lines pipe-friendly code. hood ships compiled C++ acceleration engine via Rcpp falls back gracefully fully vectorized pure-R implementation. mass_sun, mass_earth, distance_earth_sun, speed_earth built-constants — real-world values SI units (kg, meters, m/s) don’t look anything . orbitr ships constants Sun, eight planets, Moon. See Physical Constants full list. simulate_system() returns tidy tibble — one row per body per time step — ready dplyr, ggplot2, plotly, tool R ecosystem.  ’ll notice Earth’s orbit visible — Sun missing. ’s limitation plot_orbits(): draws trajectories using geom_path(), Sun barely moves simulation path small see scale. Sun move — Newton’s third law means Earth pulls Sun just Sun pulls Earth, causing trace tiny loop around system’s barycenter. ’s just invisible zoom level Sun ~330,000 times massive Earth. stellar wobble real, though — ’s exactly method astronomers use detect exoplanets. default, plot_orbits() returns standard ggplot object planar (2D) simulations, plotly HTML widget simulations 3D motion. (can also force 3D rendering planar data three_d = TRUE.) 2D case returns regular ggplot, can layer additional geoms, scales, themes, labels onto + like ggplot. One quick fix missing Sun drop marker origin: works Sun sits essentially origin throughout simulation — barycenter wobble well inside Sun . systems central body actually moves noticeable amount, ’d want pull position simulation tibble instead hardcoding (0, 0). better 2D plots control point markers, axis ranges, labels, use ggplot2 directly simulation tibble (see Custom Visualization). interactive 3D views can zoom find Sun, see 3D Plotting.
code: library(orbitr)  sim <- create_system() |>   add_body("Sun",   mass = mass_sun) |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth, x = distance_earth_sun, vy = speed_earth) |>   simulate_system(time_step = 86400, duration = 86400 * 365)  sim ## # A tibble: 1,462 × 9 ##    id       mass          x           y     z      vx          vy    vz  time ##    <chr>   <dbl>      <dbl>       <dbl> <dbl>   <dbl>       <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> ##  1 Sun   1.99e30         0          0       0   0        0            0     0 ##  2 Earth 5.97e24 149600000000       0       0   0    29780            0     0 ##  ... sim |> plot_orbits() sim |>   plot_orbits() +   ggplot2::geom_point(     data = data.frame(x = 0, y = 0),     ggplot2::aes(x = x, y = y),     color = "gold",     size = 6   ) +   ggplot2::labs(title = "Earth-Sun Orbit")

title: A Tidy Physics Engine for Building and Visualizing Orbital Simulations
text: 3D interactive plotting, ’ll also want:
code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("DRosenman/orbitr") install.packages("plotly")

title: A Tidy Physics Engine for Building and Visualizing Orbital Simulations
text: workflow simple: create system, add bodies, simulate, plot. create_system() initializes empty simulation standard gravitational constant G. add_body() places body given mass, position, velocity. Built-constants like mass_earth distance_earth_moon save looking anything . simulate_system() runs N-body integration. default Velocity Verlet integrator conserves energy stable long-term orbits. plot_orbits() produces quick 2D trajectory plot — interactive 3D plotly visualization body Z-axis motion. control, use ggplot2 plotly directly simulation tibble. output standard tidy tibble, can plug straight ggplot2, plotly, dplyr, whatever normally use.
code: create_system() |>   add_body("Earth", mass = mass_earth) |>   add_body("Moon",  mass = mass_moon, x = distance_earth_moon, vy = speed_moon) |>   simulate_system(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 28) |>   plot_orbits()

title: A Tidy Physics Engine for Building and Visualizing Orbital Simulations
text: Quick Start Guide — Full getting-started walkthrough Examples — Earth-Moon, Sun-Earth-Moon, Kepler-16, Physical Constants — built-masses, distances, speeds 3D Plotting — Interactive 3D visualization plotly Custom Visualization — Build plots ggplot2 plotly Physics — Gravitational equations, integrators, C++ engine Reference Frames — Shift perspective shift_reference_frame() Unstable Orbits — random configurations chaotic

what: Add a physical body to the system — add_body
title: Add a physical body to the system — add_body
text: function introduces new celestial physical body `orbit_system`. must provide unique identifier mass. default, body placed origin (0, 0, 0) zero initial velocity unless specified.

title: Add a physical body to the system — add_body
code: add_body(system, id, mass, x = 0, y = 0, z = 0, vx = 0, vy = 0, vz = 0)

title: Add a physical body to the system — add_body
text: system `orbit_system` object created `create_system()`. id unique character string identify body (e.g., "Earth", "Apollo"). mass mass object kilograms. x Initial X-axis position meters (default 0). y Initial Y-axis position meters (default 0). z Initial Z-axis position meters (default 0). vx Initial velocity along X-axis meters per second (default 0). vy Initial velocity along Y-axis meters per second (default 0). vz Initial velocity along Z-axis meters per second (default 0).

title: Add a physical body to the system — add_body
text: updated `orbit_system` object containing newly added body.

title: Add a physical body to the system — add_body
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ my_universe <- create_system() |>   add_body(id = "Earth", mass = 5.97e24) |>   add_body(id = "Moon", mass = 7.34e22, x = 3.84e8, vy = 1022) } # }

what: Initialize an orbitr simulation system — create_system
title: Initialize an orbitr simulation system — create_system
text: Sets foundational data structure orbital simulation. Universal N-body gravity automatically integrated system using specified gravitational constant.

title: Initialize an orbitr simulation system — create_system
code: create_system(G = 6.6743e-11)

title: Initialize an orbitr simulation system — create_system
text: G gravitational constant. Defaults standard physics (6.6743e-11). simulate zero-gravity environment (inertia ), set `G = 0`.

title: Initialize an orbitr simulation system — create_system
text: empty `orbit_system` object ready bodies added.

title: Initialize an orbitr simulation system — create_system
code: # Creates a system with standard gravity my_universe <- create_system()  # Creates a universe with 10x stronger gravity heavy_universe <- create_system(G = 6.6743e-10)  # Creates a zero-gravity sandbox floating_universe <- create_system(G = 0)

what: Physical Constants for Orbital Mechanics — physical_constants
title: Physical Constants for Orbital Mechanics — physical_constants
text: curated set real-world masses orbital distances use convenient starting points `orbitr` simulations. values SI units (kilograms meters).

title: Physical Constants for Orbital Mechanics — physical_constants
text: Numeric scalar kilograms. object class numeric length 1. object class numeric length 1. object class numeric length 1. object class numeric length 1. object class numeric length 1. object class numeric length 1. object class numeric length 1. object class numeric length 1. object class numeric length 1. object class numeric length 1. object class numeric length 1. object class numeric length 1. object class numeric length 1. object class numeric length 1. object class numeric length 1. object class numeric length 1. object class numeric length 1. object class numeric length 1. object class numeric length 1.

title: Physical Constants for Orbital Mechanics — physical_constants
text: `mass_sun`: Mass Sun (1.989 x 10^30 kg). Source: IAU 2015 nominal solar mass. `mass_earth`: Mass Earth (5.972 x 10^24 kg). Source: IAU 2015 nominal Earth mass. `mass_moon`: Mass Moon (7.342 x 10^22 kg). Source: JPL DE440 ephemeris. `mass_mars`: Mass Mars (6.417 x 10^23 kg). Source: JPL DE440 ephemeris. `mass_jupiter`: Mass Jupiter (1.898 x 10^27 kg). Source: JPL DE440 ephemeris. `mass_saturn`: Mass Saturn (5.683 x 10^26 kg). Source: JPL DE440 ephemeris. `mass_venus`: Mass Venus (4.867 x 10^24 kg). Source: JPL DE440 ephemeris. `mass_mercury`: Mass Mercury (3.301 x 10^23 kg). Source: JPL DE440 ephemeris. `distance_earth_sun`: Semi-major axis Earth's orbit around Sun   (1.496 x 10^11 m, ~149.6 million km). Earth's actual distance varies   ~147.1 million km (perihelion) ~152.1 million km (aphelion). `distance_earth_moon`: Semi-major axis Moon's orbit around Earth   (3.844 x 10^8 m, ~384,400 km). Moon's actual distance varies   ~363,300 km (perigee) ~405,500 km (apogee). `distance_mars_sun`: Semi-major axis Mars's orbit around Sun   (2.279 x 10^11 m, ~227.9 million km). Mars notably eccentric orbit   (e = 0.093), ranging ~206.7 million km ~249.2 million km. `distance_jupiter_sun`: Semi-major axis Jupiter's orbit around Sun   (7.785 x 10^11 m, ~778.5 million km). `distance_venus_sun`: Semi-major axis Venus's orbit around Sun   (1.082 x 10^11 m, ~108.2 million km). Venus circular orbit   planet (e = 0.007). `distance_mercury_sun`: Semi-major axis Mercury's orbit around Sun   (5.791 x 10^10 m, ~57.9 million km). Mercury eccentric planetary   orbit (e = 0.206), ranging ~46.0 million km ~69.8 million km. `speed_earth`: Mean orbital speed Earth around Sun (29,780 m/s). `speed_moon`: Mean orbital speed Moon around Earth (1,022 m/s). `speed_mars`: Mean orbital speed Mars around Sun (24,070 m/s). `speed_jupiter`: Mean orbital speed Jupiter around Sun (13,060 m/s). `speed_venus`: Mean orbital speed Venus around Sun (35,020 m/s). `speed_mercury`: Mean orbital speed Mercury around Sun (47,360 m/s).

title: Physical Constants for Orbital Mechanics — physical_constants
text: Orbital distances truly constant. Every orbit ellipse, separation two bodies changes continuously. distances provided **semi-major axes** — average closest approach (periapsis) farthest point (apoapsis). semi-major axis single characteristic length scale elliptical orbit: determines orbital period via Kepler's Third Law, paired circular velocity distance, produces near-circular orbit closely approximates real trajectory. example, Earth-Sun distance varies 147.1 million km (perihelion January) 152.1 million km (aphelion July). semi-major axis 149.598 million km sits right middle gives correct orbital period one year.

title: Plot Orbital Trajectories (Smart 2D/3D Dispatch) — plot_orbits
text: Plot Orbital Trajectories (Smart 2D/3D Dispatch)

title: Plot Orbital Trajectories (Smart 2D/3D Dispatch) — plot_orbits
code: plot_orbits(sim_data, three_d = FALSE)

title: Plot Orbital Trajectories (Smart 2D/3D Dispatch) — plot_orbits
text: sim_data tibble output `simulate_system()` three_d Logical. TRUE, forces 3D plot even 2D data.

title: Plot 3D Interactive Orbital Trajectories — plot_orbits_3d
text: Generates interactive 3D visualization orbital system using plotly. can click, drag rotate, scroll zoom trajectories.

title: Plot 3D Interactive Orbital Trajectories — plot_orbits_3d
code: plot_orbits_3d(sim_data)

title: Plot 3D Interactive Orbital Trajectories — plot_orbits_3d

title: Plot 3D Interactive Orbital Trajectories — plot_orbits_3d
text: plotly HTML widget displaying 3D orbits.

title: Plot 3D Interactive Orbital Trajectories — plot_orbits_3d
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ create_system() |>   add_body("Earth", mass = 5.972e24) |>   add_body("Moon", mass = 7.342e22, x = 3.844e8, vy = 1022, vz = 150) |>   simulate_system(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 30) |>   plot_orbits_3d() } # }

what: Shift the coordinate reference frame of the simulation — shift_reference_frame
title: Shift the coordinate reference frame of the simulation — shift_reference_frame
text: Recalculates positions velocities bodies relative specific target body. effectively "anchors camera" chosen body, placing origin (0, 0, 0) time steps.

title: Shift the coordinate reference frame of the simulation — shift_reference_frame
code: shift_reference_frame(sim_data, center_id, keep_center = TRUE)

title: Shift the coordinate reference frame of the simulation — shift_reference_frame
text: sim_data tidy `tibble` containing output `simulate_system()`. center_id character string ID body use new origin. keep_center Logical. central body remain dataset (0 coordinates) removed? Default `TRUE`.

title: Shift the coordinate reference frame of the simulation — shift_reference_frame
text: tidy `tibble` updated `x`, `y`, `z`, `vx`, `vy`, `vz` columns.

title: Shift the coordinate reference frame of the simulation — shift_reference_frame
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Simulate Sun-Earth-Moon orbit_data <- create_system() |>   add_body("Sun", mass = 1.989e30) |>   add_body("Earth", mass = 5.97e24, x = 1.496e11, vy = 29780) |>   add_body("Moon", mass = 7.34e22, x = 1.496e11 + 3.84e8, vy = 29780 + 1022) |>   simulate_system(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 365)  # Shift view to Earth and plot orbit_data |>   shift_reference_frame(center_id = "Earth") |>   plot_orbits() } # }

what: Simulate kinematics for an orbitr system — simulate_system
title: Simulate kinematics for an orbitr system — simulate_system
text: Propagates physical state `orbit_system` time using numerical integration. engine supports multiple mathematical methods, defaulting energy-conserving Velocity Verlet algorithm ensure highly stable orbital trajectories.

title: Simulate kinematics for an orbitr system — simulate_system
code: simulate_system(   system,   time_step = 60,   duration = 86400,   method = "verlet",   softening = 0,   use_cpp = TRUE )

title: Simulate kinematics for an orbitr system — simulate_system
text: system `orbit_system` object created `create_system()`. time_step time increment per frame seconds (default 60s). duration Total simulation time seconds (default 86400s / 1 day). method numerical integration method: "verlet" (default), "euler_cromer", "euler". softening small distance (meters) added prevent numerical singularities bodies pass close . gravitational distance computed `sqrt(r^2 + softening^2)` instead `r`. Default 0 (softening). value like 1e4 (10 km) reasonable planetary simulations. use_cpp Logical. `TRUE` (default), uses compiled C++ acceleration engine better performance. Falls back vectorized R C++ code available.

title: Simulate kinematics for an orbitr system — simulate_system
text: tidy `tibble` containing physical state (time, id, mass, x, y, z, vx, vy, vz)   every body every time step.

title: Simulate kinematics for an orbitr system — simulate_system
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ my_universe <- create_system() |>   add_body("Earth", mass = 5.97e24) |>   add_body("Moon", mass = 7.34e22, x = 3.84e8, vy = 1022) |>   simulate_system(time_step = 3600, duration = 86400 * 28) } # }
